The reason for `<=3` is that we only want to do elementwise min/max if you receive two inputs that are both tensors and of the same shape: `torch.min(input, other, *, out=None) → Tensor`, however you could also receive `torch.min(input, dim, keepdim=False, *, out=None)` where dim is an int according to the [Pytorch docs](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.min.html). 

The second branch in this conditional, checks for `len(inputs) == 2 and inputs[0].shape == inputs[1].shape`, otherwise it falls into this else case where it can still have either 2 or 3 inputs.

> users can always do it in a reversed way by providing custom collate function and call `default_collate` if needed.

That is not true. `default_collate` does two things:

1. Handle containers such as lists, tuples, dictionaries, and so on, by recursively calling itself.
2. Handle "primitive batches", by `torch.stack`'ing tensors, putting integers and floats in a list and so on.

What we need is to modify 2. without touching 1. Since these two parts are inseparable at the moment, we cannot call `default_collate` to do 1. for us, because it would do the wrong "primitive batch" handling. Thus, we have to rewrite the functionality of 1. from scratch and can only call `default_collate` for the "primitive batches" that we don't want to handle differently.

> we thought adding argument to `default_collate` might not be the ideal option

My proposal is not advocating for that. I introduced a `collate` function that only does 1. from above and is flexible on 2. The old `default_collate` will just `functools.partial` of from it and thus not need / expose any extra parameters.

We did the same fixes in onnx-mlir for the same type of vulnerability:

from https://cplusplus.com/reference/cstdio/vsnprintf/
```
Write formatted data from variable argument list to sized buffer
Composes a string with the same text that would be printed if format was used on [printf](https://cplusplus.com/printf), but using the elements in the variable argument list identified by arg instead of additional function arguments and storing the resulting content as a C string in the buffer pointed by s (taking n as the maximum buffer capacity to fill).

If the resulting string would be longer than n-1 characters, the remaining characters are discarded and not stored, but counted for the value returned by the function.

Internally, the function retrieves arguments from the list identified by arg as if [va_arg](https://cplusplus.com/va_arg) was used on it, and thus the state of arg is likely to be altered by the call.

In any case, arg should have been initialized by [va_start](https://cplusplus.com/va_start) at some point before the call, and it is expected to be released by [va_end](https://cplusplus.com/va_end) at some point after the call.

Parameters
s
Pointer to a buffer where the resulting C-string is stored.
The buffer should have a size of at least n characters.
n
Maximum number of bytes to be used in the buffer.
The generated string has a length of at most n-1, leaving space for the additional terminating null character.
[size_t](https://cplusplus.com/cstdio:size_t) is an unsigned integral type.
format
C string that contains a format string that follows the same specifications as format in [printf](https://cplusplus.com/printf) (see [printf](https://cplusplus.com/printf) for details).
arg
A value identifying a variable arguments list initialized with [va_start](https://cplusplus.com/va_start).
[va_list](https://cplusplus.com/va_list) is a special type defined in [<cstdarg>](https://cplusplus.com/cstdarg).
```

The key phrase is "If the resulting string would be longer than n-1 characters, the remaining characters are discarded and not stored, but counted for the value returned by the function."

So in your case, the code is already safe as the buffer is sized `BUFFER_MAX_SIZE` and you limit the writing in the buffer by `BUFFER_MAX_SIZE` bytes -1.

The format string is kind of irrelevant, as any `%f` or similar input will be expanded in multi-characters. And the `vsnprintf` already will truncate the output string written to the buffer to `BUFFER_MAX_SIZE`.

So you can safely remove the assert, which does not protect anything, and I would just write a lengthy comment explaining why this is safe.

If I am not mistaken, (under `mMode == kModeTrickle`)  if we have `mPhase == kBeforeRandomTime` and `mTimeInInterval < aIntervalMax` then the new calculated `newFireTime` may be incorrect. 

An example:

```
    |<------mInterval = 20 sec --------------------------->|
    |                                         |            | 
    |<------- new aIntervalMax = 15 sec ----->|            |
    |                              |          |            |
    |<-- mTimeInInterval=11 sec -->|          |            |
    |                              |                       | 
                 ^
                 Now 
```

- `mInterval = 20 sec`, `mTimeInInterval = 11 sec`,  new `aIntervalMax = 15 sec`
- Since `mTimeInInterval < aIntervalMax` we keep `mTimeInInterval` as is (will remain `11`).
- We set the `mInterval` to `15`.
- Since we are in `mPhase == kBeforeRandomTime`,  `GetLastTimerStart()` will return the start of current trickle interval
- `newFireTime` will point to 15 sec after the start of trickle interval.
- When the timer fires, we are still in `kBeforeRandomTime` phase so we do the following:
```c++
        case kBeforeRandomTime:
            // We reached end of random `mTimeInInterval` (aka `t`)
            // within the current interval. Trickle timer invokes
            // handler if and only if the counter is less than the
            // redundancy constant.

            mPhase = kAfterRandomTime;
            TimerMilli::Start(mInterval - mTimeInInterval);
            VerifyOrExit(mCounter < mRedundancyConstant);
            break;
```
Which then start the timer for remaining time to end of current interval calculated as `mInterval - mTimeInInterval = 15 - 11 = 4`, so the overall interval becomes `15 + 4 = 19` second long which is not what we want.

I think in this case, 
- The timer is already scheduled to fire at `mTimeInInterval` after start (or time `t`) which we keep the same, so we don't need to change the timer firetime.
- We only need to update the `mInterval` to `aIntervalMax` so that after timer fires, we apply the shorter interval.

This is the right time to save the energies, but you partially misunderstood the structure of the code. 

There are 2 database files : line database (loaded in df) and energy levels, either given directly or computed from spectroscopic constants. In noneq calculations we parse through the line database and, for each line, which is a transition between a lower and upper level, we look up the Energy database and assign the corresponding energy of the lower level, and the energy of the  upper level. This look-up takes time, too much time, your job is to cache the results (unless user uses a different Energy database) 

Therefore, the cache file should be created next to the Line database. Here you generated it next to the spectroscopic constants.

Assuming the test CDK app is written in Python, the test will fail if it is run in a Python environment that `aws_cdk` and other cdk dependencies used in the test projects are not installed. (Same for node.js/typescript or any other languages that CDK supports)

One approach is to add the installation scripts in `appveyor.yaml` (and any other relevant appveyor config) to ensure the dependencies are present in the appveyor environment. But we still need to install the dependencies locally when running the test locally.

An alternative approach (for the Python case) is to create a venv and install the dependencies in the venv. And then use `--cdk-app` option in the command list to instruct `cdk synth` to run within the venv.

Thinking about it more, unfortunately, this already breaks multi-language support.

Conceptually what we want is to look for a character that separates words. In English , French... this character is a space and is encoded as a single byte UTF-8 character. So this works as expected for English , French...

However for Chinese, Japanese, and Korean (CJK) there are "[ideographic spaces](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/ideographic_space)" which separate words and are _not_ represented as a single byte UTF-8 character, see [here](https://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/3000/index.htm). Thus std::isspace() and this code will not work with such ideographic spaces.

I am not sure if this is the only case that would break this code.

We need to protect against someone defining a receiver object and *forgetting* to pass in the `content` keyword. 

For example, the following code contains a terrible typo that would allow content tampering, something that the developer did not intend.

```python
request = {
  'headers': {
    'Authorization': sender.request_header,
    'Content-Type': content_type
  },
  'url': url,
  'method': method,
  'content': 'Evil unhashed content'
}

# Whoops! The developer forgot to pass in the `content` keyword. This could have been a mistake.
receiver = Receiver(
  lookup_credentials,
  request['headers']['Authorization'],
  request['url'],
  request['method'],
  content_type=request['headers']['Content-Type'])
```

I have them separately to be able to use them as part of the async definition pattern https://vuejs.org/v2/guide/components-dynamic-async.html#Handling-Loading-State - they are used outside of  `KContentRenderer` to render the loading state while renderer is loaded asynchronously and to show the error state if it fails to load.

I could probably define the loading component inline, as it is only used in the loading state handling, but I needed to set the props on the `KCircularLoader` - I could have used Vue.extend to extend the `KCircularLoader` component and set the default prop value to what I wanted here, but this seemed more explicit.

The final reason that the loading and error components are injected, rather than being part of the exported content renderer is to allow for an alternative (e.g. vuetify) loader to be used in place in Studio, or in the case of the error component for a different wrapper component (not UiAlert, which I believe we don't export) and different interface text.

So we have 3 possible strategies by now:
- The naive `getsizeof` approach, that could be incremented to look inside container objects (ever recursively, if we take care of recursive structures). This should be reasonably fast.
- The `serialize → check size → write` approach that I mentioned. This would be the slowest one, but maybe not so much as the serialization would be computed just once per object (and maybe it could be aborted midway for large objects).
- The `serialize → write → check size → roll back if too big` approach, inspired by what `logger.trace()` does. This one may or may not be efficient depending on the size of the large objects being discarded. I'm not so sure the serialization of such objects could be aborted as in the previous case. The stream would need to have a `seek()` method and framing would likely have to be turned off.

Only the first approach can be implemented as a simple filter. I would create a filter factory for it, like `ipython_filter()`:

```python
def size_filter(size):
    import re, sys
    match = re.fullmatch(r'(\d+)\s*(B|[KMGT]i?B?)', size, re.IGNORECASE)
    if not match:
        raise ValueError("invalid 'size' value: %r" % size)
    coeff, unit = match.groups()
    coeff, unit = int(coeff), unit.lower()
    if unit == 'b':
        size = coeff
    else:
        base = 1024 if unit[1:2] == 'i' else 1000
        exponent = 'kmgt'.index(unit[0]) + 1
        size = coeff * base**exponent
    def exclude_large(obj):
        return sys.getziseof(obj) > size
    return exclude_large
```

You opened pandoras box here @jni xD sorry for the long answer.

>  I was today years old when I learned that ellipses have vertices

I learned about them in secondary school, which was quite some time ago now x) Apparently, learning early is of no advantage here, since I am confusing terminology and am making things unnecessarily complicated.

> At any rate, from what I can tell here, the vertex is only a point and we are after the major axis length?

You are right that a vertex is just a point; somehow I remembered it as being the distance between the two points. 
Major and minor axes are only useful in 2D (nD would have many minor axes?); apparently the correct term is principal axis to refer to an arbitrary axis for [ellipsoids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellipsoid), and if we want the thing corresponding to radius it is principal semi-axis ... which I am certain almost nobody will make intuitive sense of.

The radius term from your second link refers to the [more general concept](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curvature) of the *(radius of) curvature of a surface at a certain point* (a good image for 1D is in the section "Osculating circle" in the link) and is a local property of a single point on some n-dimensional Riemannian manifold if you know what manifolds are. 

We can uniquely define curvature in the 1D case (ellipsis), but it will change depending on which point along the border we consider. For the ND case (we need at least 2D for the ellipsoid; it's a surface) we have to be satisfied with a curvature along a given direction. We could try choosing something that makes sense, e.g., the minimum curvature for all directions for a given point; in this case, the `radius` would be the radius of the smallest ball that we can fit to the surface of the ellipsoid. Unfortunately, this would again change based on where on the ellipsoid's surface we compute curvature, and at this point, I think we may have left the realm of "intuitive".

> I still need to get my brain straight as to why this is an ellipsoid and not the cap of a bigger sphere. Is it that you are approximating the cap as an ellipsoid? and is that approximation necessary?

I am not approximating the cap; it is because being the person writing the code and making some design decisions has it's little perks and I like making things unnecessarily complicated. 🤣 

The reason is actually related to the *radius of curvature* you brought up earlier and what that means for the level sets of the kernel. 

For the "cap of a ball" idea the kernel will have the same curvature everywhere on its surface (being a bit liberal and hand-wavy here ;) ), and it's level sets will be concentric circles around the center of the kernel. Slicing this has two pain points: (1) if the `kernel.shape` is significantly smaller than the radius then you filter with a rectangular kernel in the spatial dimensions, if it is of equal size as the radius you filter with a circular kernel, and in between you filter with a rectangular kernel with rounded edges; this is pretty weird behavior. (2) the level sets "fall off" the side of the kernel, which means that you will have uneven intensity around the border. This is not too big a problem per se, but it can introduce strange effects. For example, for `kernel.shape`s where one side is substantially larger than the other, you are computing a rolling ball filter along the long side of the kernel, but approximate a minimum filter along the short side. Again, not a huge issue, but potentially unintuitive.

For the ellipsoid idea, we are changing the curvature so that we "squash" the ball to always fit inside the kernel. The level sets never "fall off" the side, we use a kernel of same shape in all cases, and things just magically work out nicely. The downside is that you have to start thinking about ellipsoids an introduce complicated terminology . . .

> A number of alterations are also made to the sys module. All modifications to items in sys are reverted before the function returns.
>
> Note that, unlike run_module(), the alterations made to sys are not optional in this function as these adjustments are essential to allowing the execution of sys.path entries. So I'm in the process of adding it, but `train.get_dataset_shard()` requires a train session to be set up. I'd specifically like to avoid that as we want to provide a layer to run (tune) function trainables without a train layer. Starting a train session would lead us to using a BackendExecutor managing another layer of workers (or rather a single worker), etc. It generally introduces a lot of complexity just for the sake of being able to specify resources.

I think there are some possible solutions here:

1. Don't use datasets and preprocessors. Users can pass them via `tune.with_parameters` if needed or construct them in their training function. Arguably you don't need our session-guided sharding mechanisms if you're using a single worker per default.
2. Add something like `session.get_dataset()` to return the full dataset. This would have to be added to both the tune and train session, but could be guarded on the AIR level. Not ideal to clutter the tune session scope imo
3. Pass them in the `config` dict to the `train_fn` if provided
4. Add them as optional parameters to the `train_fn`, only pass them if provided (similar to the old `checkpoint_dir`)

Thinking more about this, I think the config dict approach is least invasive. Also when users are using `Tuner(train_fn, param_space={"preprocessor": tune.choice([pp1, pp2])}` it could be expected that these are passed as config arguments.

Thanks for putting together this tutorial. Just a few (random) general thoughts. 

Ultimately, I think it will be useful to have tutorials like this for each main application area (i.e., at least ecephys, ophys, and icephys) where we can then cover details that are specific to the area. E.g., for ecephys that would involve items like using ``ElectricalSeries.electrodes`` to locate and use electrode metadata. Or for icephys it involves dealing with the icephys metadata tables, for which we already have a tutorial here https://pynwb.readthedocs.io/en/stable/tutorials/domain/plot_icephys_pandas.html . As such, I would suggest that we focus here on introducing common key concepts and then link to tutorials for the different areas for further details in those areas. I think overal this tutorial is a great start for this, but we may (eventually) want to move items that are specific for a particular area to (e.g., ``units``) to the corresponding domain-specific read tutorial. 

I don't think we necessarily have to cover all these concepts here, but I just wanted to mention a few general concepts that I think could be useful for read tutorials to:
* ``Container.children`` and ``NWBFile.all_children`` to iterate over containers
* ``NWBFile.acquistion``, ``NWBFile.processing`` and ``NWBFile.all_children`` use  ``LabelledDict`` which has some (basic) features that are useful for query (see https://hdmf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/hdmf.utils.html?highlight=LabelledDict#hdmf.utils.LabelledDict). E.g., if you want to find all ``ElectricalSeries`` in acquisition you could do something like ``NWBFile.acquistion['neurodata_type==ElectricalSeries']``
* A few general concepts from the ``Container`` base class that  could be of interest for this tutorial, are, e.g,: ``type_hierarchy()``, ``.parent``, ``objectid``, ``get_ancestor``. Some of these may be too much detail or not useful here, but I just wanted to mention them in case they are useful (see https://hdmf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/hdmf.container.html?highlight=fields#hdmf.container.AbstractContainer)
* Similarly, ``hdmf.container.Data`` has a few additional features that could be useful for read, e.g., the ``transform`` method. 
* ...

someone can be calling `Graph.query(strOrQuery="SELECT * WHERE { ?s ?p ?o }")` as by default all arguments can be used as keywords or positional arguments, and if they are then changing `strOrQuery` to `str_or_query` will cause their code to fail.

Since python 3.8 there is an option for making arguments positional only [[PEP-0570](https://peps.python.org/pep-0570/)], so for example `def query(self, str_or_query, /, initBindings={}, initNS={}, DEBUG=false)` - and with a positional only arg it is impossible to call it using a keyword, but without this all args can be used as keywords.

AFAIK, a closure is a function that was parametrized and returned by another function. Thus, something like

```py
def foo(bar):
    def closure():
        return bar
    
    return closure

closure = foo("bar")
print(closure())
print(closure())
```

This would make a closure a special case of a general local function. Using a local function for the code at hand would look like

```
def test():
    def collate_fn(batch):
        return batch

    dl = DataLoader(..., collate_fn=collate_fn)
```

Going by the definition above `collate_fn` is a local function, but not a closure. This is why I wrote

> lambda's or local functions cannot be pickled

I agree Kang.  I spent more time investigating the reason for the fault, and I discovered the real issue in the documentation for mbedtls md.h:

```
/**                                                                                                                                                                  
 * \brief           This function clears the internal structure of \p ctx and                                                                                        
 *                  frees any embedded internal structure, but does not free                                                                                         
 *                  \p ctx itself.                                                                                                                                   
 *                                                                                                                                                                   
 *                  If you have called mbedtls_md_setup() on \p ctx, you must                                                                                        
 *                  call mbedtls_md_free() when you are no longer using the                                                                                          
 *                  context.                                                                                                                                         
 *                  Calling this function if you have previously                                                                                                     
 *                  called mbedtls_md_init() and nothing else is optional.                                                                                           
 *                  You must not call this function if you have not called                                                                                           
 *                  mbedtls_md_init().                                                                                                                               
 */
void mbedtls_md_free( mbedtls_md_context_t *ctx );
```

So the issue happens when mbedtls_ssl_cookie_free() (which calls mbedtls_md_free()) is called without calling mbedtls_ssl_cookie_init() (which calls mbedtsl_md_init()). 

I fixed the dtls.cpp code so that mbedtls_ssl_cookie_init() is called at the beginning of Setup(), before any failure can occur, and for either client or server.  That way in FreeMbedtls(), mbedtls_ssl_cookie_free() can be called to cleanup regardless of what happened. This fixes the fault for me.

Let me explain how this function works for us to be on the same page.
Example 1: Let's say we have 0 workers currently running, 0 pending launches, 0 pending nodes. We want to add another 100 because of `nodes_to_add_based_on_requests`. 
The functionality you describe will actually be the case and we will bypass the max concurrency launch (in that case `nodes_to_add = 5 > 0` and spin up 100 nodes.
Example 2: Let's say we have 0 workers currently running, 5 pending launches/nodes (the only difference from Example 1 is that we happen to have 5 pending nodes). We want to add another 100 because of `nodes_to_add_based_on_requests`. 
This _will not_ spin up the 100 nodes because at the current stage because `nodes_to_add = 5-5 = 0` and hence the if statement will be false.

I think this is not the behavior you expect. You would probably want to spin up the 100 nodes regardless of the pending nodes. When you do that (spin up 100 nodes regardless), you have to handle the case that the pending nodes can be failed nodes. I have the same issue when I want to perform aggressive autoscaling.

Sets are asymptotically faster with `__contains__`, but with a len-2 iterable, they're about the same. Interestingly, I suspected checking `(label == "pos") or (label == "neg")` would be faster, but it's slower on average...

```python
In [1]: def f1(label):
   ...:     return label in ["pos", "neg"]

In [2]: def f2(label):
   ...:     return label in {"pos", "neg"}

In [3]: def f3(label):
   ...:     return (label == "pos") or (label == "neg")

In [4]: %timeit f1("pos")
90.9 ns ± 1.99 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10,000,000 loops each)

In [5]: %timeit f1("neg")
109 ns ± 1.85 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10,000,000 loops each)

In [6]: %timeit f2("pos")
96 ns ± 2.38 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10,000,000 loops each)

In [7]: %timeit f2("neg")
94.2 ns ± 2.55 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10,000,000 loops each)

In [8]: %timeit f3("pos")
100 ns ± 3.85 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10,000,000 loops each)

In [9]: %timeit f3("neg")
120 ns ± 2.84 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10,000,000 loops each)
```

I think we should make this function capable of requiring a copy. Right now box wrapping (and possibly a few other places) contain extra internal logic to either make an extra copy or not, and adding in place operations makes that logic more complicated than simply adding a `.copy()` to the output of this function. I would suggest adding a new argument `copy="optimize"` that has the following options:

- `default` (better name welcome): This would match the current behavior: copy if needed. Errors if any of the shape/dimensionality checks fail, or the `np.require` call fails. Whether or not a copy is made is entirely determined by whether or not `np.require` makes a copy internally.
- `inplace`: Never makes a copy. Errors if any of the shape/dimensionality checks fail. Also errors if the input array does not have the correct flags. This can either be implemented by checking the flags directly (which this PR currently does), or by calling `np.require` and raising an Exception if the output is a copy. This method would also remove the initial call to `np.asarray` and replace it with a type check
- `copy`: Always returns a copy of the input array. Errors if any of the shape/dimensionality checks fail. Replace the call to `np.require` with a call to `np.array(array, dtype=dtype, order='C')`. 

We will want to make sure that copies are minimized. That also means that the initial `np.asarray` call should probably pass the `dtype` and `order` arguments through in the `default` and `copy` cases, if we're going to retain the call. Moreover, it's redundant to call both `np.asarray` and `np.require` the way that we do now, so we can simplify that if we figure out the exact right numpy function that makes copies in the situations we want.

def _raise_if_bad_http_status_and_method(response):
    # type: (ResponseType) -> None
    """Check response status code is valid.

    Must be 200, 201, 202, or 204.

    :raises: BadStatus if invalid status.
    """
    code = response.status_code
    if code in {200, 201, 202, 204}:
        return
    raise BadStatus(
        "Invalid return status {!r} for {!r} operation".format(
            code, response.request.method
        )
    )

def initialize(self, client, initial_response, deserialization_callback):

    ...

    except BadStatus as err:
        self._status = "Failed"
        raise HttpResponseError(response=initial_response.http_response, error=err)

The worthless try/catch in this block is for the tracer system not anything meaningful to the user.  In the case where I can't catch an error in the debugger and I have to find all the cases each object was born and died I have to use the tracer.  Unfortunately the JP_TRACE_OUT pattern implies that it will rethrow the exception which makes some C++ compilers unhappy because a extern "C" code can't have an exception.  Thus the next safest code to use the tracer is JP_PY_TRY/JP_PY_CATCH which should appear in all extern "C" Python calls.   I know that it ends up with some really odd code because it appears like we are handling exceptions in routines that don't have any C++ code or exceptions.   It also does one other function, which is these blocks can serve as failure points for instrumentation.   Yes, I release we have a lot of stuff hooked up to those macros (tracing, error handling, and instrumentation) but splitting them out just makes the code blotted and less readable.

Of some interest: you have probably seen us write `.collect()` before to
turn an iterator into a vector. But here we’re turning one into a hash
set! In fact, [`collect`] can generate any collection type, as long as
it implements [`FromIterator`]. The specific type in any instance is
determined by context, and the compiler will complain if there’s not
enough information to uniquely identify a type. This is sometimes called
[target typing].

[`collect`]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/trait.Iterator.html#method.collect
[`FromIterator`]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/trait.FromIterator.html
[target typing]: https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/java/javaOO/lambdaexpressions.html#target-typing

Ah, I see your point. I was reading a similar formulation in "Basic Steps in Geostatistics: The Variogram and Kriging" (Oliver and Webster) in Equation 4.11.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10455520/82937057-5f149b80-9f44-11ea-9e01-0e580195b9f4.png)

and comparing this with (Wolfram Rühaak, 2014)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10455520/82937401-f7128500-9f44-11ea-9b98-7425bc1263ba.png)

PyKrige look like its been implemented in the (Wolfram Rühaak, 2014) way, so I think we want to subtract it like you asked, if I'm understanding this correctly. But I _think_ these negatives are redundant.

In the implementation of solving the system to get the linear estimators in `PyKrige` it looks like `b` on the RHS is set to be negative, https://github.com/GeoStat-Framework/PyKrige/blob/1a889a7f087ac7c3e852f6e48601f4a60efca260/pykrige/ok.py#L649 

When finding λ = inv(A) b, the contents of A and b are defined in (Wolfram Rühaak, 2014) and in PyKrige
https://github.com/GeoStat-Framework/PyKrige/blob/1a889a7f087ac7c3e852f6e48601f4a60efca260/pykrige/ok.py#L635, as being negative, meaning λ is positive. 

But in 4.11 in Oliver and Webster, it looks like they've defined the contents of A and b to be positive, which to me seems like the variance/nugget parameter would be added to the diagonal to make the solution for the system of equations (λ = inv(A) b) consistent between both methods, and ensure λ is positive.

So I would expect equivalent behaviour between subtracting the nugget with A and b having negative entries and adding the nugget with A and b having positive entries.

Here is the behaviour from subtracting the nugget, instead, with PyKrige as is:
![PyKrige_NE_Interp](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10455520/82948408-94c28000-9f56-11ea-8350-55cbe882bdd8.png)

Which produces some strange variance, perhaps originating from this added term to the variance from Oliver and Webster, Equation 4.12:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10455520/82948884-8032b780-9f57-11ea-9f23-dda5c52205ae.png)

So perhaps https://github.com/GeoStat-Framework/PyKrige/blob/1a889a7f087ac7c3e852f6e48601f4a60efca260/pykrige/ok.py#L622

Needs to have another term if using PyKrige as a non-exact interpolator? In either case, yes, its rather confusing. Does this make sense to you?

If we're switching to requiring setuptools, let's make sure to avoid the main reason we haven't required setuptools: that it breaks `python setup.py install` by implicitly doing egg installs and corrupting sys.path:

```python
from setuptools.command.bdist_egg import bdist_egg

class bdist_egg_disabled(bdist_egg):
    """Disabled version of bdist_egg

    Prevents setup.py install from performing setuptools' default easy_install,
    which it should never ever do.
    """

    def run(self):
        sys.exit(
            "Aborting implicit building of eggs. Use `pip install .` to install from source."
        )

...
setup_args['cmdclass'] = {
    ...
    'bdist_egg': bdist_egg if 'bdist_egg' in sys.argv else bdist_egg_disabled,
}
```

Answering my own question... when the partner knows the secret via the SecretReveal message but the transfer hasn't been unlocked, we keep checking on every block until it becomes unsafe to wait then we invoke registering the secret on-chain.
- In this code path, the transfer hasn't been unlocked as the current target task still exists.
- Also in this code path, the secret is known (see: target.handle_block).
So if what the above doing is:
-  Check if partner knows the secret. If so, and it's not safe to wait, then register the secret on-chain.
What should happen is:
- Check if partner knows the secret, it's not safe and wait and that the secret hasn't been already registered on chain... if so, register the secret on-chain.

After adding words, at some point, the user will ideally want to sort the dataset based on the frequency. The most frequent word should be the index 0, the second most frequent index 1, etc. - This is a memory optimization. Words follow [Zipf's law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zipf%27s_law). When you read in a cacheline of data from this lookup you're most likely going to read index 0. Since you always read 64 bytes, that'll result in certain number of the first few words to always be read. The probability of hitting an index close to 0 is very high due to Zipf's law, so on the second read you'll likely be able to use the data you just read into cache. If the indicies on the other hand are random you'll constantly trash cache for a single word and read much more than you need to.

On that note, using std::string is probably not a good idea, because you need tight control over the structure that's used to represent the word. We can however add these optimizations later on once you have a benchmark (please add that as well) and can look at the memory pressure of a typical read. A good benchmark is tokenizing and creating a dictionary based on Wikipedia. At some point we might also want to align an entry struct or use SmallVector to store the first few characters on the stack.

According to the definition of `ValidationError` ([here](https://github.com/Julian/jsonschema/blob/v3.0.1/jsonschema/exceptions.py#L131)) the exception does have information related to the validator that caused the issue (ie. type mismatch), path, etc. 

By raising a new `ValidationError` exception we're preserving the type of the raised exception but we're going to miss the additional contextual information provided by `jsonschema` library.

My recommendation here would be to catch the exception, on the caller side, to do something meaning-full with it and to modify the exception message in order to enhance the reported message (modify the `message` attribute of the `ValidationError` instance).


PS. As you have the operation object available in the method I would suggest to mention the `operationId` that caused the exception such that it will be even easier to spot the exact call that caused the issue (ie. `"{0} for header '{1}' of operation '{2}'".format(e.message, header_name, op.operation_id)`)

Pytest does runtime opcode rewriting. That is how we get these nice reports on failed asserts:

```
>        assert a == b
E       assert 1 == 2
```
The important thing here is that it shows the value of `a` and `b`. But this is only done for the test modules, imported modules are not rewritten and the test report is like this:

```
>       assert a == b
E       AssertionError
```

This `register_assert_rewrite` allows opcode rewriting for additional modules. I'm using it to get better error reports for our assert utilities

The `RpcError` can pass through other error codes from gRPC as well, not only the `UNKNOWN` error. The ones I have seen being used downstream as part of this PR:

```
UNAVAILABLE
UNKNOWN (both of the above will lead to the client retrying)
DEADLINE_EXCEEDED (I think this is basically a timeout)
RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED ("message too big")
```

I don't have strong opinions, but ideally I think we would have a well-defined set of RPC error messages that we expose to clients that are interpretable and clear to handle, so we don't lock ourselves into gRPC implementation details. This is however out of the scope for this PR, I was just trying to do the minimal amount of changes to keep the downstream code working here :) cc @scv119 

Let me know if you think there is a better strategy to handle things for this PR that is still incremental!

I do have a follow up PR that exposes the error codes used today in the Python coede through Cython as well so we can get rid of more of the grpcio package .

```

is really python-specific and the same should probably be achieved by doing

dumps((0, *range(1, 4), 4))

The example about dumping multiple objects IMHO should also be treated differently. With Splice it would create an asymmetry: there would be a way to dump multiple objects, but not to read them back. @LuDuda , the relatively recent PR https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/3484 brought the SLAAC feature support into core. Before this CLI build enabled support for SLAAC but NCP did not actually support it (`wpantund` did handle SLAAC if was needed by application). 

My main intention with this PR is to allow us to control SLAAC behavior and make it consistent with original model (e.g., have a way to disable SLAAC fully on an MTD build to save memory/code size). 

For this change, I earlier considered if we can somehow have SLAAC be enabled by default for CLI builds but not for NCP builds (to be consistent with the original model). But since this feature is now part of core, we need to decide if to enable/disable the feature when building core ot library (which can then be linked (shared) in both NCP and CLI builds). So no easy/clean way to add such a condition.

At the end, I went with the above model: i.e., disable by default and for it to be explicitly enabled if needed. I think it's safer to be explicit about support of SLAAC and as you mentioned, it follows all other features in OT. We explicitly enable it in all example makefiles.

Something to think about for the future:
When you are writing deeply nested code, consider ways you can simplify it. There are usually ways to write the same logic with less control flows or splitting the nesting into simpler private functions. So in this case, if we could default `self._build_images` to an empty dict in `__init__` then we safe ourselves the need to do `None` checks (we do this throughout the code base for this reason). 

When acting on dictionaries in python, try to avoid using `[]` to access elements and instead use `.get()` method. The `.get()` method does the checks you want for you. So you can simplify both inner if statements by doing `image = self._build_images.get(layer_name)`. By default, this will return `None` if the element (`layer_name`) is not in the dict.

Ah yes, you are right - I just checked this out locally. The issue here is that the `@patch("kolibri.core.tasks.api.queue")` decorator on the TestCase does not seem to be mocking anything - the other tests in this test case all pass, as they all pass in the case that a job is not found.

I think the issue is due to the fact that the TaskViewset `queues` property is set to a list of `[queue, priority_queue]` because this is defined at module parse time, the mocking fails to intercept this, because at the time that `[queue, priority_queue]` is defined the references are to the original, non-mocked objects.

The easiest way to make this work would probably be to turn the `queues` property of the TaskViewset into:
```
@property
def queues(self):
    return [queue, priority_queue]
```

This change along with passing `job_id=2` instead of `id=2` (my mistake), causes the tests to now pass.

I get the no tasks case (and if that's the only case then maybe this should be in an `else:` block to make that clear), but if an operator does have tasks, I don't see how the last execution of the `while tasks` loop could consume all of the available operator outputs, and then have more operator outputs available right after exiting that loop, without any new operator event or state change.

>but other operators can have arbitrary behavior

Well, they can't have completely arbitrary behavior; the executor needs to have some contract around when it can expect the operator to have new outputs, and when it can expect the operator's output production to be finished. E.g. one sensible contract between the executor and the operator would be:
1. If an operator has 1 or more tasks, then it only produces more outputs after a task completes (i.e. after `op.notify_work_completed()` is called).
2. If an operator has 0 tasks, then it has a static set of outputs.

For (1), we try to consume the operator outputs whenever a task finishes; for (2), we do a single pass of operator output consumption. Whatever the contract, I'm just arguing for making it as explicit as possible in the executor code, so devs (like me) aren't left wondering "wait why would we need this last operator output consumption if the operator had tasks, shouldn't it have been exhausted at the end of the task loop?"

I'd prefer to keep our interfaces as consistent as possible. We already have a Format enum; I think we can just use that directly and let Redshift sort out whether the passed format is acceptable or not. It would give us forwards-compatibility if AWS implements additional formats from that existing enum in the future.

While the SQLAlchemy API methods per se are not specific to CrateDB, I found this section worth to keep, to display different kinds of inspection and reflection methods of SQLAlchemy together with their slightly CrateDB-specific outcomes, also because they are not mentioned elsewhere in our documentation (modulo `has_table`), and also because the corresponding spots are a bit obfuscated within the SQLAlchemy documentation.

In other words, while bringing those snippets together from different parts of our documentation, I have made an extra effort to discriminate between the different kinds of introspection/reflection features offered by SQLAlchemy:

- SQLAlchemy inspector
- Schema-supported reflection
- CrateDialect-specific reflection methods

The result is a reasonably concise overview about them, paired with CrateDB-specific responses.

```python
>>> inspector.get_schema_names()
['blob', 'doc', 'information_schema', 'pg_catalog', 'sys']

>>> inspector.default_schema_name
'doc'

>>> dialect.has_schema(connection, 'doc')
True
```

We need to get rid of this because I believe there's no clean way to allow manifest imports with first-class remotes. If nrf/west.yml defines a remote R with url-base-A and imports zephyr/west.yml, which defines R to have url-base-B, there's no clean way to do this as an "override".

Projects in zephyr/west.yml are completely "inlined" *before* being imported into nrf/west.yml. At that point, the remotes disappear and the URL is what really matters.

If you look at the model for how manifest imports work here, you can see that only a subset of project data is considered for merging when we model them as tuples:

file:///home/mbolivar/zp/zephyr/doc/_build/html/guides/west/manifest.html#west-manifest-formal

The 'remote' is not part of that.

I did it this way because if we have both `remote` and `url` attributes in a west.yml entry for a project, there's no clear way to decide which one should win if a sub-manifest uses one, and a parent manifest uses the other.

So I basically "erase" the remotes after parsing each manifest and just override the URLs.

Because at that point two symbols (functions) exist with the same name. The user code defines one set (with `STB_IMAGE_IMPLEMENTATION`, used exactly once as you're supposed to) and your copy-pasted code defines the other. If the dynamic library doesn't export these symbols (on windows it shouldn't, on Linux and OS X it's possible to do it but I don't know if that's been done here) then no problem. If does or if using static library then duplicate symbols error happens.

Two ways to fix this:
1. Rename your copy-pasted symbols so none of them clash. Leads to duplicate code, makes it hard to update it and can bloat the executable.
2. Replace your code with the real `stb_image.h`, include one object file where you define `STB_IMAGE_IMPLEMENTATION `. Document that it's in there so users aren't caught unaware. Provide CMake option to disable it in case users want to use theirs.

Yes, things can break. Nothing is permanent. People can use old versions of conda, or they can hotfix repodata. We won't do these things lightly, but they need to be possible.

This particular change is really not too important to split hairs over, and I'm honestly happy to ignore this as cruft, but there's a lot of old stuff in old packages that genuinely breaks things. If we are bound completely by the past, conda will not be able to move forward. There are sane limits to backwards compatibility. Docker containers and/or VMs should also be a part of your long term reproducibility strategy. Conda's primary concern is keeping things working correctly, not necessarily keeping old things working. Where those are at odds, old stuff will break.

> Not entirely sure I get the connection how to support glob usage with a sources env var..?

If you adjust the example in the PR to use globs, you'll see an issue:
```
sources=["*.txt"],
command="cat *.txt",
```
... won't work for a few reasons:
1. glob expansion usually happens in a shell, and `cat` won't have one here. So it will fail to find a literal file named `*.txt`
2. even if the process being invoked is `bash`, bash's glob expansion will not be equivalent to our own (which tries to match `git`'s syntax).

Having said that! I just realized that there is a bit of a dilemma here. Directly invoking `cat` still wouldn't work with an env var containing a list of sources... because env vars are expanded by shells. All of this might be why Bazel went with requiring that the top level process be `bash`? I could imagine having our own sort of templating/placeholder syntax other than env vars though, maybe...?

It is in Pex injected in the `__main__.py` in a `--venv` mode PEX file: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/pull/1153/files#diff-8018b797310f9896a6574a746376acaf0730b409d6fe03ab73887f823240505cR57

When you create a `--venv` mode PEX you can run the PEX file itself like normal and it will start up using the `__main__.py` and minimal `.bootstrap/` code in the PEX zip file in order to check to see if has already created a venv under `~/.pex/venvs`:
a. If it has, it re-execs itself against the venv.
b. If it hasn't, it seeds the venv and runs from there.

This makes using the PEX file transparent and robust but costs 60ms of overhead over and above a raw venv. I.E.: option a requires ~60ms to check and re-exec.

One way to get around this is to manually create a venv from the PEX once, i.e.: `PEX_TOOLS=1 ./pytest_runner.pex venv pytest.venv` and then store `venv pytest.venv` as an output directory and run that venv for pytest executions. The issue with that is it introduces ~500 ms overhead extra in chroot creation time by the engine / loading time by python / the page cache.

For example:
```
$ hyperfine --warmup 2 './pants --pex-create-explicit-venv test --force src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex_cli_test.py' './pants test --force src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex_cli_test.py'
Benchmark #1: ./pants --pex-create-explicit-venv test --force src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex_cli_test.py
  Time (mean ± σ):      2.412 s ±  0.065 s    [User: 524.8 ms, System: 55.3 ms]
  Range (min … max):    2.285 s …  2.503 s    10 runs
 
Benchmark #2: ./pants test --force src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex_cli_test.py
  Time (mean ± σ):      1.878 s ±  0.070 s    [User: 621.3 ms, System: 79.4 ms]
  Range (min … max):    1.753 s …  1.964 s    10 runs
 
Summary
  './pants test --force src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex_cli_test.py' ran
    1.28 ± 0.06 times faster than './pants --pex-create-explicit-venv test --force src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex_cli_test.py'
```

That's with some changes over in PEX to support creating venvs that use no symlinks and precompile all .py files in the entire venv:
```diff
diff --git a/src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex.py b/src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex.py
index 0ea56f37a..07401ec2b 100644
--- a/src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex.py
+++ b/src/python/pants/backend/python/util_rules/pex.py
@@ -767,11 +767,164 @@ def wrap_venv_prex_request(pex_request: PexRequest) -> VenvPexRequest:
     return VenvPexRequest(pex_request)
 
 
+@frozen_after_init
+@dataclass(unsafe_hash=True)
+class PexProcess:
+    pex: Pex
+    argv: Tuple[str, ...]
+    description: str = dataclasses.field(compare=False)
+    level: LogLevel
+    input_digest: Optional[Digest]
+    extra_env: Optional[FrozenDict[str, str]]
+    output_files: Optional[Tuple[str, ...]]
+    output_directories: Optional[Tuple[str, ...]]
+    timeout_seconds: Optional[int]
+    execution_slot_variable: Optional[str]
+    cache_scope: Optional[ProcessCacheScope]
+
+    def __init__(
+        self,
+        pex: Pex,
+        *,
+        description: str,
+        argv: Iterable[str] = (),
+        level: LogLevel = LogLevel.INFO,
+        input_digest: Optional[Digest] = None,
+        extra_env: Optional[Mapping[str, str]] = None,
+        output_files: Optional[Iterable[str]] = None,
+        output_directories: Optional[Iterable[str]] = None,
+        timeout_seconds: Optional[int] = None,
+        execution_slot_variable: Optional[str] = None,
+        cache_scope: Optional[ProcessCacheScope] = None,
+    ) -> None:
+        self.pex = pex
+        self.argv = tuple(argv)
+        self.description = description
+        self.level = level
+        self.input_digest = input_digest
+        self.extra_env = FrozenDict(extra_env) if extra_env else None
+        self.output_files = tuple(output_files) if output_files else None
+        self.output_directories = tuple(output_directories) if output_directories else None
+        self.timeout_seconds = timeout_seconds
+        self.execution_slot_variable = execution_slot_variable
+        self.cache_scope = cache_scope
+
+
+@dataclass(frozen=True)
+class CopiedVenvScriptWriter:
+    pex: Pex
+    venv_dir: PurePath
+
+    def _create_venv_script(
+        self,
+        bash: BashBinary,
+        pex_environment: PexEnvironment,
+        *,
+        script_path: PurePath,
+        venv_executable: Optional[PurePath] = None,
+    ) -> VenvScript:
+        env_vars = (
+            f"{name}={shlex.quote(value)}"
+            for name, value in pex_environment.environment_dict(python_configured=True).items()
+        )
+        target_venv_executable = shlex.quote(str(venv_executable)) if venv_executable else ""
+        script = dedent(
+            f"""\
+            #!{bash.path}
+            set -euo pipefail
+
+            export {" ".join(env_vars)}
+
+            HERE="$(cd "$(dirname "${{BASH_SOURCE[0]}}")" && pwd)"
+
+            exec ${{HERE}}/{self.venv_dir}/bin/python {target_venv_executable} "$@"
+            """
+        )
+        return VenvScript(
+            script=Script(script_path),
+            content=FileContent(path=str(script_path), content=script.encode(), is_executable=True),
+        )

The other way to get around it is to use `--seed` mode where PEX prints out the path to the seeded venv. That allows you to execute the seeded venv directly, skipping the PEX file overhead, but it open you up to the world changing when someone nukes the `~/.pex/venvs` cache out from under you.

As such, for now, the only option to get raw venv speed is `--venv` `--seed` and a wrapper script to repair the `--seeded` venv when it disappears. The wrapping bash script only costs ~1ms.

This is fine, feel free to leave as is. I just wanted to give you an option to use an alternative approach, if you are dissatisfied with the current one, and would like to avoid the casts and the need for the comment.

The crux of the problem is that `mypy`'s static analysis is insufficiently powerful to see that `right_is_binary_composition == True` iff `isinstance(expression.right, BinaryComposition)`. This is true because:
- `right_is_binary_composition` is initialized with that precise statement on line 167 (in the new numbering), and
- `right_is_binary_composition` is never altered anywhere between that point and this point.

The easiest way to address this limitation of `mypy`'s is to make `left_binary_expression: Optional[BinaryComposition]` and `right_binary_expression: Optional[BinaryComposition]` variables, which are set to `expression.left` and `expression.right` if that side is a `BinaryComposition`, and `None` otherwise. Essentially, this is a type coercion operation, and in many languages, this operation is implemented via an `as` operator: `left_binary_expression = expression.left as BinaryComposition` would be the pseudo-Python way to express it here.

Then, the assignments on lines 197/199 don't need a cast, because they would read `expression_to_rewrite = left_binary_expression` which is valid no matter what, as the types of the two variables are definitionally the same.

Feel free to use your best judgment in deciding whether this alternative would be more or less readable than the current version.

```
import timeit
set_up_line = """
import numpy as np
import torch as pt
pt.manual_seed(1)
np.random.seed(1)
vocab_slice_idss = np.random.randint(0, 10, (100, 50))
target_prefix = pt.randint(0, 10, (100, 50))
"""
executive_line = """
z = target_prefix.device
for i in range(target_prefix.size(0)):
    vocab_slice_ids = np.lib.arraysetops.unique(np.concatenate([vocab_slice_idss[i], target_prefix[i].cpu().int().numpy().flatten()]))
    vocab_slice_ids = pt.nn.functional.pad(pt.tensor(vocab_slice_ids, dtype=pt.int64, device=z),  # type: ignore
                                           pad=(0, 7 - ((vocab_slice_ids.size - 1) % 8)),
                                           mode='constant', value=2)
"""
timeit.timeit(executive_line,setup=set_up_line, number=1000)
6.065197644056752


executive_line = """
z = target_prefix.device
for i in range(target_prefix.size(0)):
	vocab_slice_ids = pt.tensor(vocab_slice_idss[i])
	vocab_slice_ids = pt.unique(pt.concat([vocab_slice_ids.type(pt.int64).to(z), target_prefix[0].type(pt.int64).to(z)], -1))
	vocab_slice_ids = pt.nn.functional.pad(vocab_slice_ids.clone().detach(),  # type: ignore
	                                           pad=(0, 7 - ((vocab_slice_ids.size(-1) - 1) % 8)),
	                                           mode='constant', value=2)

timeit.timeit(executive_line,setup=set_up_line, number=1000)

I thought about it, but unfortunately there is no good way in Python to hint to the interpreter that the particular method is always going to throw an exception. Thus linters cannot properly predict the code flow after such method is called, because they cannot guess the correct flow. Example


```

def method_that_always_raises():
    raise ValueError


def some_other_method():
        method_that_always_raises()
        # here linters are lost and assume the second operator would still be executed
        assert False, 'This never happens'


def method_that_returns_exc():
    return ValueError


def some_other_method2():
        raise method_that_returns_exc()
        # here linters know for sure that method execution would never reach the next line
        assert False, 'This never happens'
```

We'd discussed offline whether the name of the input property to the provider should be `serverURL` or `pluginDownloadURL` and decided to go with the latter. The latter is more verbose, but it aligns with the [name of the property in the schema](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/c95e49e5abc9ea845f08475a3a44bcf9ea593942/pkg/codegen/schema/schema.go#L1520) and is less likely to conflict with a provider's own input properties (which very well could have a `serverURL` for its own purposes). This PR does use `pluginDownloadURL` for the input property itself, but everywhere else in the implementation is using `ServerURL`.

Two questions:

1. Should we use `PluginDownloadURL` throughout instead of `ServerURL` to be consistent and keep the concept count lower for easier readability/understandability of the code?
   - I realize we do use `ServerURL` in the [`workspace.PluginInfo`](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/c95e49e5abc9ea845f08475a3a44bcf9ea593942/sdk/go/common/workspace/plugins.go#L93) struct and `Server` for the [`plugin.PulumiPluginJSON`](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/eb735c7106fcbb48eb3e0edaab3d0baea47d2f0e/sdk/go/common/resource/plugin/plugin.go#L58) struct, so there is a case to be made for `ServerURL`.
1. When we expose this in the SDKs on `ResourceOptions`, do we want to expose it as `ResourceOptions.PluginDownloadURL` or `ResourceOptions.ServerURL`?

Curious if anyone else has a preference.

> Why there are 2 distinct buttons to add a case to a group? If you click several times on the one on the bottom you find yourself in this situation, which is a bit weird

Thanks a bunch for the review! Super to see what is not obvious to others.

The underlying reason is that a case should be able to join multiple groups, to allow for different investigations, like one with a related family, one with cancer remission sample, one with another tumor or sth. Also, groups don't get made on load (yet?) so they have to be made through the interface at some point.

There are three kinds of buttons, and in my simple mind they form a nice minimum, though perhaps we can come up with better names for them:
- "Add group" adds a new group (unnamed, can add names to see if that helps make it more clear) - and it is assumed the current case is always going to be in that new group, so it is automatically bound into the group. Groups are after all only shown on the case pages if the case belongs to the group. Perhaps it could be named "Create group"?
- "Add to group" adds other cases to the selected group. We agree on this one. 😸 Perhaps it could be named "Add other case" or such?
- "X" on group list item cases removes the case from the pertinent group. If no cases are left in the group, the group is deleted. If there are still other cases in the group, but present case A is removed, it just leaves the group, and the group is no longer visible on the case page of A. It will still be visible from the other group pages.

On my system, I tried this implementation vs. `np.pad` for x.shape = (2048, 2048) and kernel.shape = (101, 101).

`np.pad` (from NumPy 1.19.0) took 6.33 ms ± 84 µs while the version proposed here takes 11 ms ± 84.5 µs. 

You can improve the version here a little by using `np.full` instead of `np.Inf * np.ones`, but to get performance equivalent to NumPy, I have to basically replicate what numpy is doing, which is an n-dimensional equivalent of the following 2d code:

```Python
def const_pad(x, kernel_size):
    padding_amount = kernel_size // 2 * 2
    half_pad = padding_amount // 2
    img = np.empty(x.shape + padding_amount, dtype=x.dtype)
    img[half_pad[0]:-half_pad[0], half_pad[1]:-half_pad[1]] = x
    img[:half_pad[0], :] = np.Inf
    img[-half_pad[0]:, :] = np.Inf
    img[half_pad[0]:-half_pad[0], :half_pad[0]] = np.Inf
    img[half_pad[0]:-half_pad[0], -half_pad[0]:] = np.Inf
    return img
```

That `const_pad` function gives nearly identical time to `numpy.pad` for me.

Yeah, getting the feeling I didn't think this through all the way.  There was a question about latency introduced and buffer sizes from the client and was hoping we could come up with a way to size the buffer before the messages arrive while allowing us to pass through the messages.  Obviously if we sent the signature ahead of the messages we could do this easily but it'd mean we'd need to hold these messages in a buffer, generate the signature, then send the signature message ahead of the message group.

One way to do this might be to assume a signature buffer with a fixed maximum size (that is, it'd always be sized to hold 44 messages).  Each block in the buffer would be 255 byte blocks that would be filled by messages.

To find where a message would slot into the buffer we could use the CRC has a hash value, then use open indexing with linear probing to find where to slot the message: https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/implementing-hash-table-open-addressing-linear-probing-cpp/.

This means we'd always be signing a buffer with a fixed size, and many of the bytes would be zero in the signature buffer for smaller groups.

This would require always having 11220 (44*255) bytes for the sig buffer, but this is the upper maximum that would be needed anyway.

Oh, this magic `ClientConnection::Create` is critical to maintain the lifetime of this object (it is extremely confusing, but I remember it is from boost official examples). One example is here:

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/fcdf410ae1bb5071e7d92174eace7d79be2d4ef9/src/ray/raylet/raylet.cc#L129-L148

In this example, it looks like `new_connection` will go out of scope, and we are leaking memory. But actually `ClientConnection::Create` calls `client_handler`, and `client_handler` will call `ClientConnection::ProcessMessages()`, then `ClientConnection::ProcessMessages()` will general a `shared_ptr` of itself via `shared_ClientConnection_from_this()` in a boot::asio callback, so that its lifetime will be only controlled by `boost::asio`. This is quite tricky, but there is hardly other ways to achieve it.

Not specifically related to this PR, but just want to have more a general discussion on the static callback methods.

Recently we are moving towards defining them in header file instead of the `cpp` file.

I see little benefit in doing this and actually may be some limitations. I suggest we define them in `cpp` file.

- These `static` methods are used as callbacks and we use their address (as a function pointer) passed as callback to some other object. So they cannot be inlined, so having them in the header should not help with toolchain inlining them.
- With the ones that use `GetOwner<>()` method, we may hinder the toolchain inline optimization (with some older toolchains). An older toolchain may not be able to inline the template method implementation if it is not visible/known at the point that it's being used.
   - Note that `locator.hpp` defines the template method `GetOwner<>` without an implementation
   - And the implementation is in `locator-getters.hpp`.
   - The idea is that OT core header files `#include <common/locator.hpp>` and then the `cpp` files `#include <common/locator-getters.hpp>`
   - This is to avoid the loop dependencies of header files. Note that `locator-getters.hpp` needs to include `instance.hpp` which itself includes every other OT core header file. So we cannot directly use `locator-getters.hpp` from other OT core header files.

I didn't notice any bugs being introduced, but I went back and removed the useless changes to print / log lines to not clutter the diff with unrelated changes.

For reference:

- `f'{foo}'` uses the `__format__` method from `foo`
- pathlib objects do not override `__format__`, so `f'{pathobj}'` ends up calling `object.__format__` on `pathobj` with an empty `format_spec`
- [`object.__format__`](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__format__) with an empty `format_spec`  ends up calling `str()` on the object being formatted (with some differences between python 3.6 and later versions that don't matter to us)
- for pathlib objects, `os.fspath(pathobj)` and `str(pathobj)` are the same thing, because `__fspath__` delegates to `str()`
- `west.log` ends up calling `print()` on its arguments, which will print them out just like `str()` would format them

So TL;DR `west.log.inf(f'blah blah f{pathobj}')` and `west.log.inf('blah blah ', pathobj)` produce the same results, because `__format__` for these objects works like `str()`, which works like `os.fspath()`, so there's no differences in printing them with f-strings or as separate args, they always produce the right filesystem representation as a string, and we might as well not introduce pointless churn.

Where this *does* matter is in places like west.manifest, which accept `os.PathLike`. In the general case, `str(some_os_PathLike)` and `os.fspath(some_os_PathLike)` are *not* the same, because the first uses `__str__` and the second uses `__fspath__`, which don't need to be the same (though they are for pathlib).

That's why I tried to use os.fspath in places in project.py where we were not obviously just printing things out.

while i generally agree, i think it's good to stay consistent with established patterns.

it is common throughout IO implementations in different platforms (including Java, and all others I know), that, if you pass an underlying resource to some higher-level abstraction that closable, you're yielding control over that resource to the abstraction. for instance, in this piece of code:

```python
from io import BufferedReader

my_raw_stream = ...

with BufferedReader(my_raw_stream) as reader:
    reader.read()
```

the BufferedReader will call `close()` on `my_raw_stream`.

even though the case may be a bit different, i would treat it the same way. i agree that the drawback is that you cannot re-use session objects across clients. but if you really want to do that, you can always patch the `close` method to a no-op.

Overview of changes in this particular function:
- Change `filter_fields` to `filter_field`. The function previously incorrectly assumed that if an index covers the `filter_fields`, then this filter is very selective. This is not the case since `filter_fields` are loosly defined, and can mean anything. In the case of `in_collection` and `=` filters, there's always just one filter field, so the wrong codepath was actually never called.
- Previously this function was a helper for `=` filters that was also used in estimating `in_collection` selectivity. We can't do that anymore since we dropped the assumption that the filter value doesn't matter. We need to look at all values inside the collection. So now we compute `=` filter selectivity using this helper by passing a single-element list for the `collection` argument.

So you are pointing out to the core aspect of our CoroutineExecutor. Indeed `await` corresponds to `yield from`. async/await are actually plain generators with `yield from` wrapped into another syntax. But Python2 does not have support for `yield from`. But since we are already doing our own CoroutineExecutor, we can handle `yield core` with the same semantic as if it was `yield from coro`. This is handled by CoroutineExecutor here:

https://github.com/zopefoundation/ZEO/blob/0d0e16ab4490adf6aa3c8549b634b89baee83f4e/src/ZEO/asyncio/futures.py#L303-L306

in a sense we are implementing support for `yield from` ourselves.

This gives ability to follow the same style async/await is using with just a bit different syntax.

the run if it has empty data. But then a missing time series is

TensorBoard.dev we don’t keep track of empty time series, but there’s no
such restriction in the data provider model,\* so I don’t see why we
should go out of our way to lose information here. Can we just drop the

structure of the data provider methods already has a simple, lossless
way to keep track of which time series exist and whether they have data,
and that plugin code should try to stick to that when possible. In most
cases this should generally amount to just doing the straightforward
thing without extra conditions.

\* If you want a motivating example, imagine some tuning service that
knows ahead of time what metrics you want to track.

What if `steps.datasets.conclusion == 'failure'` and `steps.transforms.conclusion == 'success'`? `success()` will be `false` since we have at least one failure. 

In this case `success() || steps.transforms.conclusion == 'failure'` boils down to `false || false` -> `false` and the step is not run. 

With my version we get `false || true || false` -> `true` and the step will be run.

In general we need an expression that makes sure that the setup has succeeded. This is achieved by checking `success()` or failure of any of the steps afterwards. 

Branching is a pain in CI in general, but I think with these three conditions it is somewhat tolerable. Given that this sparked quite a bit of confusion maybe we can refactor this a little. I see two options there:

1. Change the condition to `if: steps.$LAST_SETUP_STEP.conclusion == 'success'`.
2. Add a another dummy step after the setup that we can check for success

From these two I like 2. better since then we only need to remember to keep it as a last step of the setup but can do whatever we want before it.

def _get_num_columns(booster, n_features, **kwargs):
        rng = np.random.RandomState(777)
        test_data = rng.randn(1, n_features) # one-row dataset
        test_predictions = booster.predict(
            xgb.DMatrix(test_data), validate_features=False, **kwargs

        num_columns = (
            test_predictions.shape[1] if len(test_predictions.shape) > 1 else 1
        ) # getting of columns number
        return num_columns
```

```
# v56
- entries that are still in the form of fragments
# v56
- entries you've generated on release
```
but the goal of this change is to have something like
```
# v57.dev0
- entries that are still in the form of fragments
# v56
- entries you've generated on release
```

So that next version preview should either be generated here in sphinx or you should modify your release flow to change the hardcoded version to `v57.dev0` right after the release.

Ohhh you are right. So, those `Feature`s are primitive objects? I thought it's going to be a collection object that needs to handle dict or list specially at the outer level.

> I introduced a `collate` function that only does 1

I agree on adding this `collate` function to handle all collection objects before the primitive objects. But, in terms of how to register collate function for a certain primitive type of objects, I still don't agree on suppressing `TypeError`. IMHO, It's error-prone. Like [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/f64857189d514a662dac09ca6421e1e95e87e843/torch/utils/data/_utils/collate.py#L148-L149), we do want to raise `TypeError` that will be silently suppressed by the `with contextlib.suppress(TypeError):`.

So, I think prefer the following two options:
1. Add this `collate` function with  an argument of `Dict[Type, collate_fn]` to handle each object based on the type
    - First check if `type(elem)` in the dict
    - Second go through each key in dict and check `isinstance` for derived class
2. Keeping the existing `default_collate`, but before any collate on primitive objects, we try to use `collate_fn` attached to the class.

In terms of JIT, I don't know if any of the above option would work. To make JIT working, a jittable custom collate function might be the best option to you.

imo this test has never been particularly useful, since it tests the specific implementation of the hash, and it's not really verifiable that it is right. What matters is that it behaves predictably with semantics that lockfile consumers can rely upon.

At this point though, there are useful semantics that we can test.

* Test that `digest([A, B]) == digest([B, A])`
* Test that `digest([]) != digest([A])`
* Test that `digest([A, B]) != digest([A, B, C])`
* Test that `digest([A, A]) == digest([A])` for identical `A` :)

I think that just about covers it.

It means we always use the left labels to align dataframes:

https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/bd326f1c4175102489f08d271a53cf374bd9125e/modin/core/dataframe/pandas/dataframe/dataframe.py#L1312-L1314

This is different from a `left` join because it uses the left index and columns exactly, whereas `left` join can change the order even if the binary operands have the same labels in different orders.

e.g. 

```python
import pandas as pd

df1 = pd.DataFrame([[2, 1]], columns=[1, 0])
df2 = pd.DataFrame([[2, 1]], columns=[0, 1])
# does not use df1 columns:
print((df1 + df2).columns)
# Uses df1 columns:
print(df1.where(df1 == 1, df2))
```

According to https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-github-hosted-runners/about-github-hosted-runners#supported-runners-and-hardware-resources, the VMs we get from GitHub Actions come with a single 2-core CPU. This is why I chose `-j2`.

All that said though, I think most of the benefit for CI comes from moving from single-threaded to multi-threaded, and the difference between `-j2` and  `-j4` shouldn't be large in that environment. If the recipes are correctly setup, conda would only allow a consistent environment.

For optional dependencies, there isn't a consistent syntax on conda. One way to do this (more recently) has been to use the `run_constrained` which means that if the package is not mandatory, but if it is installed, it should have specific constraints on it.

https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html?highlight=run_constrained#constraining-packages-at-runtime

The truth is, that many packages aren't always "built right" the first time. Which means often you find yourself with an old package version that doesn't have the specific lowerbound or upperbound constraints on conda. But these issues are often resolved in two ways:
1. A new package is released with updated dependencies, and the documentation can be updated to include `conda install "scikit-image>=0.20.0"` which then uses newer packages.
2. People stick with their old, but working environments.

This part isn't done yet, but the plan is to output annotations in the schema that includes macro edges that make it clear which edges are real vs macro-based:
```
type Foo {
    name: String
    out_Foo_Bar: [Bar]
    out_Foo_MacroBar: [Bar] @macro_edge
}

I need to clarify that with the hybrid approach people often use some of the sphinx directives (`autofunction`?) that may extract parts of the content from docstrings while having accompanying prose in the documents. I think you need to try creating more documents and follow https://diataxis.fr just to have a feeling of how this all works. When you experiment with these things more, maybe it'll become easier to judge where to use prose and what to leave fully autogenerated. Per my observations, the manually crafted parts are usually ones that describe the interconnection between things and the public API while the internal objects/reference may be more generated. I think that in terms of Diataxis, it's reasonable to have separately-maintained Tutorials and How-To Guides while Explanations and References may get more of the generated stuff. Still, it's a balance that you need to find empirically.

Nope 🚀 We run this for the user ([link to relevant lines](https://github.com/FirefoxMetzger/imageio/blob/68819d27a669364daba53e271e4583868c81b335/imageio/config/plugins.py#L101-L102)).

Roughly, what happens now is 

```python

import imageio as iio 

# modules imported up to this point
# - imageio
# - numpy

iio.imread("some.tiff")  # tifffile is automatically imported here

# modules imported up to this point
# - imageio
# - numpy
# - tifffile

iio.imread("imageio:chelsea.png")  # pillow is automatically imported here

# modules imported up to this point
# - imageio
# - numpy
# - tifffile
# - pillow

iio.imread("some.bogus")  # automatically imports and tries all plugins listed in `iio.config.known_plugins`
# raises IOError

# modules imported up to this point
# all of them ... we had to load them to try our best to read `some.bogus`
```

The above introduces a one-time cost for each plugin's first `imread`, but that can be avoided by your suggestion, i.e., if the user manually calls `import imagio.plugins.pillow` then the one-time cost for loading the plugin is paid there instead of being paid during `imread`.

On the flip side, if a user never uses `imageio-ffmpeg`, then he/she will never pay the cost of importing it (nor will he/she ever face exceptions because that plugin doesn't work on their machine). It also avoids us doing the "is this backend installed" dance inside a plugin. The plugin simply assumes that it is installed, and if it isn't it fails with an ImportError, which `imopen` picks up and handles appropriately.
